narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 66.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is below the typical student's 3.21; your LSAT score of 21, much lower than the average 36.7; and your rankings in the bottom decile for both first-year and third-year school grades, compared to averages around the middle of the pack. Your upper-middle family income is a bit above average, you're planning full-time studies like most applicants, and as a Black male, your demographic aligns with smaller but present groups in the applicant pool. When the model weighed these factors, your third-year school decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction firmly toward a higher risk of bar failure because it was so low relative to peers. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which similarly signaled challenges ahead due to its significant shortfall from the norm. Your first-year decile came next in influence, again highlighting early academic struggles that the model saw as a continued pattern. Your family income level followed, with its upper-middle status not providing enough of a boost to offset the other concerns. Your racial background as Black was the next key factor, contributing to the risk assessment in line with historical patterns in the data. Your undergraduate GPA rounded out the top influences, as its below-average mark reinforced doubts about exam readiness. Less impactful but still leaning toward risk were your plans for full-time enrollment and your gender as male. Overall, no single factor dooms you, but the combination of low academic deciles, LSAT, and GPA created the biggest hurdles compared to successful applicants. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can change: retake the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework if possible, and build a stronger academic record in any ongoing studies. With targeted preparation like bar exam prep courses or tutoring, students in similar spots have turned things around—keep pushing, as your determination can shift the odds.
